privacy.noAds: No ads or hidden tracking: Atlas Places is for collecting places, without profiling.
privacy.localLocation: Atlas Places is open source: the code is public, reviewable, and improvable by the community.
privacy.optionalAccount: Visits and notes stay on this device.
privacy.openData: Suggested corrections can improve the shared dataset after review.

export.title: Export and import
export.subtitle: {{visitsLabel}} and {{photosLabel}} ready to back up with Atlas Places.

community.title: Community and license
community.body: Atlas Places is a community project built to last. The app will live as long as users help enrich stores, correct details, add photos, and keep the dataset useful for everyone.
community.license: Public code under the GPLv3 license.

profile.deleteWarningMessage: This action is irreversible. Visits and local settings will be cleared from this device.
profile.deleted: All local data has been deleted.

cache.title: Image cache
cache.hint: Controls how many store photos are kept on disk between sessions. A larger cache means fewer network requests when revisiting stores.

romanized.title: Romanized names
romanized.hint: Show store names in romanized alphabet (e.g. Apple Taipei 101 instead of Apple 台北101).

store.notFoundBody: This store is no longer available in the local dataset.
store.visitInvalidDate: Use a date formatted as YYYY-MM-DD.
store.shareText: I visited {{name}} in {{place}} on {{date}}. Tracked with Atlas Places.
store.shareIntro: Send an Atlas Places card, a link, or a ready-to-go message to plan the visit with a friend.
store.shareFailed: Sharing could not be opened. Try again in a moment.
store.shareCardFailed: The image card could not be generated. The text message is still available.
store.shareNoImage: No shareable image is available for this store. The text message will open instead.
store.shareCardTagline: Collect Apple Stores with Atlas Places.
store.privatePhotoSaved: Photo saved locally in Atlas Places.
store.submitFailed: The submission failed. Please try again in a moment.
store.changeSubmitted: Thanks, the correction was sent for review.
store.changeRequired: Tell us what needs changing and the correct information to use.
store.suggestEditHelp: Describe the information to correct in plain language. You do not need to know the technical field name.
store.photoPermissionDenied: Allow photo access to choose an image.
store.photoSubmitted: Thanks, the photo was proposed. It will be reviewed before appearing in Atlas Places.
store.photoHelp: Add a photo you took. Your name or nickname can be shown with the photo if it is accepted.
store.fieldPathPlaceholder: E.g. address, hours, name, status, architecture...
store.contributorNamePlaceholder: E.g. first name, nickname, or group
store.photoCreditPlaceholder: E.g. first name, nickname, or group
store.caption: Caption

architectureDetails.eraFallback: {{value}} describes a design period or family used in the Atlas Places dataset. The record can still be expanded with sources, renovation dates, and detail photos.
architectureDetails.typologyFallback: {{value}} describes the store's overall form: its setting, relationship to the street or mall, and the kind of retail experience it supports.

glassCubeFlagship.body: This era covers landmark flagships where the structure itself becomes an urban signal. Glass, transparency, and monumental stairs turn the entrance into both a public marker and a store threshold.
glassCubeFlagship.title: Glass Cube Flagship

originalRetail.body: Apple's earliest stores established the core retail language: simple circulation, product tables, Genius Bar service, and a highly readable interior. They mark Apple's move into a tightly controlled physical experience.
originalRetail.title: Original Retail

townSquare.body: The Town Square era reframes the store as a civic place: Forum, video wall, planting, seating, and Today at Apple. Recent flagships aim less for pure display and more for integration with the city, heritage buildings, or public space.
townSquare.title: Town Square

unknown.body: The exact design era is not documented in Atlas Places yet. The record keeps verified elements and can be improved with a reliable source or a photo that clarifies the design generation.
unknown.title: Unknown

classic.body: A historical Apple Store type, often in a mall or shopping street, with a simple facade, product tables, and service toward the back or side. Its priority is clear customer flow.
classic.title: Classic

classicUpgrade.body: A classic store updated with newer cues: warmer materials, clearer service zones, and sometimes a Forum or video wall. It keeps the original footprint while moving the experience closer to current formats.
classicUpgrade.title: Classic Upgrade

historicUrbanFlagship.body: An urban flagship inserted into a heritage building. The architectural task is to preserve facade, volumes, or decorative details while introducing Apple's contemporary language in a legible and reversible way.
historicUrbanFlagship.title: Historic Urban Flagship

mallStore.body: A store embedded in a shopping center. Its architecture must attract people from a busy gallery, organize entry quickly, and remain legible within a constrained width and depth.
mallStore.title: Mall Store

nsd.body: NSD points to new-generation stores: more open facades, Avenue, Forum, natural materials, and clearer separation between discovery, service, and Today at Apple sessions.
nsd.title: New Store Design

standalonePavilion.body: A freestanding pavilion conceived as a complete architectural object. It can use transparency, roof form, views, and surrounding public space with a more iconic presence than a mall unit.
standalonePavilion.title: Standalone Pavilion

urbanFlagship.body: A large city store designed as a destination. It combines visibility, capacity, service areas, and sometimes multiple levels to become a marker in its district.
urbanFlagship.title: Urban Flagship

unknown.body: The typology is not fully qualified yet. The displayed attributes and notes still help interpret the record, but a correction can refine the exact format.
unknown.title: Unknown

vintageD2.body: An older format tied to intermediate generations of Apple Retail design. It remains very functional, before Forums, Avenues, and recent video walls became widespread.
vintageD2.title: Vintage D.2

vintageE.body: An older or transitional store type, useful for spotting locations that have not fully adopted the vocabulary of contemporary Apple Stores.
vintageE.title: Vintage E

watchShop.body: A format dedicated to Apple Watch, usually more compact and closer to a jewelry or accessories space. It emphasizes presentation, try-on, and direct advisor interaction.
watchShop.title: Apple Watch Shop

avenue.body: The Avenue is the wall-based display zone introduced with new-generation stores. It presents accessories, services, and collections like an interior street, with a more editorial rhythm than a simple shelf.

boardroom.body: The Boardroom is a quieter space for business customers, training, or appointments. It reflects Apple's move toward longer service relationships, not only product sales.

forum.body: The Forum hosts Today at Apple and group demonstrations. Historically, it shifts part of the store from showroom logic toward cultural programming and learning.

geniusBar.body: The Genius Bar is the historic marker of Apple Store service. Even when the specific furniture disappears in newer formats, Atlas Places flags it when the location offers or offered an identifiable support zone.

glassCube.body: The glass cube is one of Apple Retail's best-known signatures. It turns the entrance into a minimal urban object, often above a below-grade or highly transparent sales floor.

greenWall.body: The green wall softens the interior and belongs to the Town Square vocabulary. It creates a visual landmark, improves atmosphere, and connects the store to the idea of an indoor plaza or garden.

historicFacade.body: A historic facade means the store is inserted into an existing building of heritage interest. The project balances conservation, restoration, and contrast between older details and contemporary intervention.

outdoor.body: An outdoor entrance connects the store directly to a street, plaza, or forecourt. It changes the experience: the store becomes an urban address rather than only a gallery unit.

pickup.body: Pickup marks the rise of online order collection. It clarifies an increasingly important logistics flow without turning the whole store into a pickup counter.

plaza.body: The plaza is the public or semi-public space associated with the store. It extends the Town Square idea: waiting, meeting, attending an event, or simply passing through.

trees.body: Trees inside or around the store humanize large volumes and create continuity with the street. In recent stores, they are part of the public-place vocabulary rather than isolated decoration.

videoWall.body: The video wall structures the Forum and makes sessions, launches, and demonstrations visible. It is a key element in the move from product store to event and learning space.

notes.closedAppleStoreImportedFromTheAppleWiki: Closed Apple Store imported from the Apple Wiki historical opening-date table.
notes.closedAppleWatchShopImportedFromTheUserP: Closed Apple Watch shop imported from the user-provided Apple retail storelist.
notes.distinctiveSphericalStoreOnMarinaBay: Distinctive spherical store on Marina Bay.
notes.flagshipBelowAPublicPlazaWithTheDistinct: Flagship below a public plaza with the distinctive Fifth Avenue glass cube entrance.
notes.formerAppleStoreLocationImportedFromTheA: Former Apple Store location imported from the Apple Wiki historical relocation markers.
notes.oneOfTheFirstTwoAppleRetailStoresOpenedI: One of the first two Apple retail stores opened in 2001.
notes.parisFlagshipInARestoredHaussmannEraBuil: Paris flagship in a restored Haussmann-era building.

hours.official-link-only: Hours are not copied into Atlas Places because they change frequently.
hours.community-verified: Community-verified hours are available, with freshness metadata.

editor.requiredFields: Please fill all required fields: ID, name, city, country, and address.
